title: The Gender Trap: Parents and the Pitfalls of Raising Boys and Girls By Emily W. Kane. New York University Press. 2012. 297 pages. $23 (paper)
abstract: Emily Kane begins this book with a reflection on her attempt at gender-neutral parenting in the midst of “traps” that make this effort daunting: our social world is structured to highlight the differences between girls and boys, as Kane saw in such mundane experiences as the narrow selection of “princess” or “little toughie” themes when shopping for children's clothing. And she witnessed some of the everyday consequences of gender-neutral parenting in, for instance, the social exclusion her son faced as a result of not having the toys unofficially required for playing with the other boys at his preschool. The rest of Kane's book explores this “gender trap”—“a set of expectations and structures that inhibit social change and stall many parents' best intentions for loosening the limits that gender can impose on us” (3)—in a social scientific manner. The Gender Trap reports the conclusions Kane reaches after interviewing 42 fathers and mothers, each of whom had at least one preschool-age child at the time of the study. Kane's sample includes mothers and fathers who were single, heterosexually partnered, or same-sex partnered as well as members of various class and racial groups. Some of these parents happily reproduced normative conceptions of gender as they socialized their children, while others actively resisted them. Kane creates a typology of parents, based largely on where they fall on this continuum of reproduction/resistance, and she organizes her book into chapters each focused on one of the five types. In each, Kane identifies the parents' perspectives on the source of gender differences (biological or social); whether they viewed these differences as positive or negative; whether they recognized the connection between gender and power; the degree to which they reproduced and/or resisted normative conceptions of gender; and their demographic characteristics.
